QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_63

question.text: What determines whether a commodity market is in contango or backwardation?

answer.text: If storage cost + interest cost > convenience yield, the market is in contango (F > S). If convenience yield > storage cost + interest cost, the market is in backwardation (F < S)
Only supply and demand determine whether markets are in contango or backwardation
Commodity markets are always in contango because storage costs are always positive
Government price controls determine contango or backwardation

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The cost-of-carry model: F = S × e^(r + s - c)t where r = interest, s = storage, c = convenience yield. When (r+s) > c: F > S (contango). When c > (r+s): F < S (backwardation). High convenience yield (tight supply) drives backwardation; low convenience yield (ample supply) supports contango. **The Trap:** While supply-demand affects convenience yield, the formal relationship involves all three costs. Storage-only markets (no convenience yield) are always in contango. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series XVI Ch3 covers commodity carry dynamics. Financial futures (equity, currency) don't have storage costs or convenience yield — their contango/backwardation is simpler. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Commodity traders read contango/backwardation as a market signal: persistent backwardation = supply tightness; persistent contango = ample supply. This guides physical and derivatives trading.
